description: This figure presents comprehensive mechanical and physical property characterization of the developed low-density steel (LDS, Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B) compared against conventional lightweight steels. Panel (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves where the present LDS (red curve) achieves superior mechanical properties with a yield strength of 503 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 672 MPa, and uniform elongation of 13%, representing 22% and 30% improvements in strength over the conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel (orange curve: 389 MPa yield strength, 549 MPa UTS, 11% elongation) without sacrificing ductility, while the interstitial-free (IF) steel (black curve) shows lower strength but higher elongation. Panel (b) maps specific yield strength (SYS) versus uniform elongation (UE), positioning the LDS (red star) in the optimal upper-right quadrant among diverse alloy systems including Fe-Mn-Ti-B, Fe-Ti-B-(Nb), Fe-Cr-(Ni)-B, and Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. Panel (c) plots specific modulus against the product of SYS and UE, demonstrating that the LDS achieves a high specific modulus of 32.7 GPa g⁻¹ cm³ alongside favorable mechanical performance indices, distinguishing it from both ceramic-reinforced steels and traditional low-density steels. Panel (d) provides the comprehensive legend identifying the various lightweight steel categories compared. The figure collectively establishes that the heterogeneous dual-phase microstructure design enables the LDS to overcome the typical strength-ductility trade-off of ceramic-reinforced steels while maintaining exceptional specific stiffness comparable to light metals.
